title: The Config Pipeline Today: Hygiene or Architecture?

diagnosis.label: Diagnosis
diagnosis.description: What the code actually says, and what's wrong

pipeline.label: Pipeline Reality
pipeline.description: How config actually flows, compile vs runtime

exploration.label: Exploration
exploration.description: The oRPC question — open, not committed

slides.explanation:
**The question:** would a good number of our current problems be solved by better engineering practices and removing dead/redundant code — rather than a big architectural shift?

**The verdict, from reading the code:** yes. The compile/runtime split is sound — config is fully resolved at compile time and the executor is config-blind. Every problem found is hygiene-class: one over-general abstraction applied universally, a pair of dead runtime fields, convention drift in op folders, and redundant compile passes.

items.label: Ops with exactly one strategy
items.subtitle: The strategy union envelope is ceremony for ~90% of ops

items.label: Strategies using the normalize hook
items.subtitle: And one of the two is a no-op stub

items.label: Config logic at runtime
items.subtitle: Executor never normalizes or validates — by design

items.label: Findings requiring architecture change
items.subtitle: All five are remove / collapse / lock-down fixes

slides.explanation: Five authoring levels, one compile funnel. Everything flows through `compileRecipeConfig` (packages/mapgen-core/src/compiler/recipe-compile.ts:55) at compile time; the runtime path carries the baked result and never touches it again.

layers.title: What each level contributes to config

layers.label: Recipe
layers.description: createRecipe wires stages and delegates: compileConfig → compileRecipeConfig; instantiate slots compiled config into structural steps (recipe.ts:352). Only a presence check, no shape logic.

layers.label: Stage
layers.description: normalizeStrict on the stage surface, then stage.toInternal({ env, stageConfig }): splits knobs, applies knob defaults, runs the authored compile for public stages (stage.ts:194).

layers.label: Step
layers.description: prefillOpDefaults injects op defaultConfig for omitted keys, then optional authored normalize, re-validated for shape preservation. 12 of 53 steps use it.

layers.label: Op
layers.description: createOp builds a normalize that routes on config.strategy to the selected strategy's hook (op/create.ts:80). Pure plumbing — ops declare no config logic of their own.

layers.label: Strategy
layers.description: The only level that can transform its own config — and only 2 of 87 ops actually do. The other 183 strategy files legally omit the hook.

slides.explanation: The compile loop per step: prefill op defaults → strict pass 1 → `step.normalize` → strict pass 2 → op/strategy normalize → strict pass 3. The compiler enforces that `normalize` is shape-preserving — a hook that returns something off-schema fails with `normalize.not.shape-preserving` (recipe-compile.ts:194).

diagram.content:
flowchart TD
    A["recipe.compile(env, config)"] --> B["compileRecipeConfig"]
    B --> C["per stage: normalizeStrict + stage.toInternal"]
    C --> D["per step: prefillOpDefaults"]
    D --> E["normalizeStrict (pass 1)"]
    E --> F["step.normalize(config, { env, knobs })"]
    F --> G["normalizeStrict (pass 2: shape-preservation)"]
    G --> H["normalizeOpsTopLevel → op.normalize → strategy.normalize"]
    H --> I["normalizeStrict (pass 3)"]
    I --> J["CompiledRecipeConfig → instantiate → compileExecutionPlan"]
diagram.caption: All config resolution is compile-time; the plan carries baked config

slides.explanation: Runtime is deliberately config-blind. `compileExecutionPlan` copies `step.config` into plan nodes untouched, and the executor hands it straight to `step.run`. Nothing re-validates: there is no runtime validation surface at all — op `run` only checks that `config.strategy` is a string. Trust-the-compiler is the contract.

blocks.explanation: **The catch:** a hand-built `RunRequest` reaches `step.run` with completely unvalidated config, because the plan compiler holds the registry (and each step's schema) but never uses them to validate `node.config`. That's a deliberate-looking line drawn accidentally — see the dead-fields finding.

slides.explanation: **Finding 1 — the single-strategy envelope.** Every op config is a discriminated union `{ strategy, config }`, even when there is exactly one strategy. 78 of 87 ops have only `default`. For them, the union has one member, the synthesized dispatch routes among one option, and `normalizeStrict` does union-candidate search work per op key — a 1-candidate search.

The envelope tax leaks upward: step `normalize` hooks must pattern-match the discriminant just to adjust their own op's defaults. The capability is real — 9 ops in hydrology and ecology have genuine alternative strategies (e.g. `coastal-shelf`, `orogeny-boosted`) — but the SDK already hard-requires a `default` strategy on every contract, which is an admission that single-strategy is the norm. **Collapse direction:** a non-union fast path in `createOp` for single-strategy ops; keep the envelope only where alternatives exist.

blocks.explanation: Three lines of intent (multiply three weights by knob-derived factors) wrapped in seventeen lines of envelope unwrapping. Multiply by every knob-aware step in the recipe.

slides.explanation: **Finding 2 — dead runtime surface.** `MapGenStep.configSchema` and `MapGenStep.normalize` are declared on the runtime step interface and faithfully populated when the registry is built — and then never read by anything. The compiler uses the *authored module's* normalize directly; the executor and plan compiler never look at either field. They are worse than dead: they look like a runtime safety net and aren't one.

blocks.explanation: **Two honest options:** delete both fields from the runtime contract (normalization is a compile-time concern, period), or make `compileExecutionPlan` actually validate `node.config` against `configSchema` so hand-built RunRequests stop bypassing validation. Both are small. The current state — carried but inert — is the only wrong choice.

slides.explanation: **Finding 3 — convention drift in the op folders.** The SDK's own stated convention (the TODO below is in the code) is: config math goes in `normalize`, not `run`. Two ops violate it, one op ships a self-aware no-op stub, and the helper folders have three spellings and eight empty files.

rows: Drift,Where,Fix
Config clamping inside run(),plan-volcanoes + plan-island-chains strategies,Move into normalize — or encode as schema minimum/maximum which these clamps mostly duplicate
No-op normalize stub,select-resource-sites/strategies/default.ts:84,Delete; 183 other strategies simply omit the hook
Three folder spellings,policies/ (5 ops) vs policy/ (2 ops) vs rules/ (53 ops),Pick one name; none is an SDK construct — all plain helper functions
Empty template residue,8 ecology ops with rules/index.ts containing only export {},Delete the files
Schema placement drift,7 ops with separate config.ts; rest inline in contract.ts,Unify on one convention
table.caption: Convention drift inventory — all mechanical fixes

slides.explanation: **Finding 4 — redundant compile work.** The shape-preservation *guarantee* is good engineering. Recomputing it unconditionally is not: strict passes 2 and 3 run even when no `step.normalize` and no strategy `normalize` exist — which is the overwhelmingly common case (12 of 53 steps; 2 of 87 ops). Each pass redoes unknown-key search, clone, default, clean, and full error enumeration, with union-candidate search for every op envelope property.

rows: Redundancy,Location,Cost
Up to 4 normalizeStrict passes per step,recipe-compile.ts:90 + :160 + :186 + :217,Pass 2 and 3 run even when no hook ran; each does full schema walk with union-candidate search
Defaults applied twice via two implementations,buildValue: applySchemaDefaults then Value.Default (compiler/normalize.ts:126),Two codepaths owning one concern; pick one
Two different exported types named StepModuleAny,recipe-compile.ts:31 vs normalize.ts:12,Same name; different shapes; both exported from one package
Unused error channel,OpConfigInvalidError caught at normalize.ts:259; no thrower in mod code,Dead-ish surface; remove or document the intended thrower
table.caption: Compile-path redundancy — semantics unchanged by fixing any of these

blocks.explanation: Cheapest fix with the clearest win: skip strict pass 2 when `step.normalize` is absent and strict pass 3 when no op-level normalize ran. Pure compile-time cost reduction; the guarantee stays intact where hooks actually exist.

slides.explanation: The ranked ledger. Each item is one of: remove dead code, collapse a redundant abstraction, or lock down a footgun. None changes the architecture.

objectives.title: Collapse the single-strategy envelope
objectives.description: A non-union fast path in createOp when strategies has one key (or auto-wrapped default). The 9 real multi-strategy ops keep the union. Highest leverage: deletes authoring noise from 78 op configs and every envelope-aware step normalize.

keyResults.text: Union envelopes drop from 87 ops to the 9 with real alternatives
{ strategy: 'default' } boilerplate leaves recipe configs and step hooks

objectives.title: Delete — or activate — the dead runtime fields
objectives.description: MapGenStep.configSchema and MapGenStep.normalize are populated and never read. Either remove them from the runtime contract, or use configSchema in compileExecutionPlan to validate node.config.

keyResults.text: engine/types.ts:28-29 and the writes at recipe.ts:230-233
(alternative) plan-time config validation for hand-built RunRequests

objectives.title: Lock down normalize-only config logic
objectives.description: The SDK shape is right; enforcement is missing. Fix the two offenders, delete the stub, and add a guard so the drift cannot recur.

keyResults.text: Lint/test banning config math called from strategies/*/run

objectives.title: Sweep template residue
objectives.description: Folder hygiene: empty rules/index.ts files, the no-op normalize stub, three spellings of the helper folder, duplicate StepModuleAny type names.

keyResults.text: 8 export {} files; the stub; one of the two StepModuleAny types

objectives.title: Make re-validation conditional
objectives.description: Skip strict passes 2/3 when no normalize hook ran; pick one default-application implementation in buildValue.

keyResults.text: Schema passes per step in the common no-hook case: 4 → 2

slides.explanation: **The exploratory question — not a committed direction:** what if domain ops became oRPC routers, each op a procedure, with middleware handling policies? The repo already contains the strongest possible reference: `@civ7/control-orpc` uses effect-orpc with a contract tree mirrored by an implementer tree, and — crucially — uses middleware for *domain policy*, not just transport: a readiness **precondition gate** and a proof-boundary **postcondition gate**, both keyed on per-procedure declarative `.meta({ risk })`. And `createRouterClient` already proves these routers run in-process with no HTTP hop (the CLI calls the control router that way today).

diagram.content:
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAPGEN["mapgen today"]
        D["domain (ecology, hydrology, ...)"]
        O["op = contract + N strategies"]
        P["policies/ (7 tiny pure predicates)"]
        N["normalize (compile-time)"]
    end
    subgraph ORPC["civ7-control-orpc precedent"]
        R["modules/{domain}/ sub-router"]
        PR["contract procedure + one .effect handler"]
        MW[".use(readiness) .use(proofBoundary)"]
        X["(no analogue: per-call only)"]
    end
    D -->|clean| R
    O -->|"clean (contract); mismatch (N strategies)"| PR
    P -->|clean| MW
    N -->|mismatch| X
diagram.caption: Mapping mapgen constructs onto the in-repo oRPC patterns

blocks.explanation: Per the oRPC docs, middleware composes via `.use()`, can extend context through `next({ context })`, and can inspect input and output — which is exactly how the proof-boundary middleware validates handler *output* after `await next()`. So "policy as middleware" is real, documented, and already practiced in this repo. Today's mapgen policies are 7 tiny pure predicates — nothing locks us in, and nothing forces the move either.

slides.explanation: The honest mapping table, then the answer to the original question.

rows: Mapping,Verdict,Why
op → contract procedure,Clean,Both sides are TypeBox contracts with input/output; the toStandardSchema adapter oRPC needs already exists twice in-repo
domain → sub-router,Clean,modules/<domain>/{contract; router; procedures} in control-orpc mirrors mapgen's domain layout exactly
policy → middleware,Clean,mutation-readiness (precondition) and proof-boundary (postcondition) are policy-as-middleware running in production code
in-process execution,Clean,createRouterClient calls routers with no HTTP; the CLI does this today
strategy → ???,Mismatch,oRPC binds exactly one handler per contract; N named implementations dispatched on config has no counterpart
(input; config) signature,Mismatch,oRPC procedures take input only; config and defaultConfig are first-class members of OpContract
compile-time normalize,Mismatch,Middleware runs per-call; nothing in oRPC runs at recipe-compile time
sync hot loop,Mismatch,Every effect-orpc handler is async; mapgen-core has zero effect dependency; Effect docs recommend boundary adoption — not per-tile hot loops
table.caption: Four clean mappings, four real mismatches — the mismatches cluster on the execution hot path

blocks.explanation: **Bottom line.** The current problems are hygiene, not architecture: do the five-item ledger first. The oRPC idea has genuine substance — but at the *seam*, not the core: exposing the op catalog as a contract-only oRPC tree (catalog, visualization, remote invocation, policy middleware at the boundary) is a worthwhile bounded spike. Replacing the executor's synchronous hot loop with per-call async procedures is where the mismatches concentrate, and nothing in the diagnosis demands it. Sequencing bonus: the cleanup ledger — especially collapsing the single-strategy envelope — shrinks exactly the surface any future migration would have to carry.
